baseStepTitles: Attach the top and add hinges
flags: DEFECT FIXED IN PROSE: the 'Add the aprons' step specified 3/4" screws. The only fastener in materials is 'Pocket-hole screws, 1-1/4" and 2-1/2"', and a 3/4" screw through a 3/4" apron holds nothing. Routed to 1-1/4", matching the plan's own stated rule.

DEFECT FIXED IN PROSE: the cut step's board tally read 'two 8 ft 1x4, one 8 ft 1x3 and one 8 ft 1x4' — three 1x4 boards against the two declared. packBoards confirms all eight 1x4 parts (48, 2x15-3/4, 2x12, 2x16-3/4, 23-3/4) fit on two 8 ft boards. Tally corrected to two.
Only ONE 48" 2x2 bottom support exists in the cut list, but the source step says to join the leg sets 'front and back' with it. Placing it at the back is DERIVED, not authored.
The height of the 2x2 bottom side supports on each leg set is not specified in the source; the rewrite says to mark both sets together rather than naming a height.

Verified sound and used: 48" + two 1-1/2" legs = 51" frame, 15-3/4" + two 1-1/2" = 18-3/4" depth, against a 51-1/2" x 19-1/4" top = 1/4" overhang all round. 48" - two 12" front aprons = 24" drawer opening. Box geometry solver: drawer sides (16") run full, front/back (21-1/2") sit between, box 23" outside, proved by the 16" x 23" bottom — so 1/2" slide clearance per side in the 24" opening, and the 23-3/4" face leaves 1/8" per side.

ops.body: Cut four legs at 29-1/4", two bottom side supports at 15-3/4" and one bottom support at 48" from the 2x2. Cut two top side supports at 15-3/4" and two top front/back supports at 48" from the 1x2. Cut one back apron at 48", two side aprons at 15-3/4", two front aprons at 12" and two drawer supports at 16-3/4" from the 1x4. Cut two drawer sides at 16" and two drawer front/back at 21-1/2" from the 1x3. The 1/4" plywood is used whole as the 16" x 23" drawer bottom. Rip the drawer face to 3" wide and cut it 23-3/4" long from a 1x4 offcut, and cut the tabletop to 51-1/2" x 19-1/4". Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 2x2, one 12 ft 1x2, two 8 ft 1x4 and one 8 ft 1x3 — the drawer face packs onto the same two 1x4 boards as the aprons, so no third 1x4 is needed. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw; several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

Each leg set is two 29-1/4" legs joined by one 15-3/4" 2x2 bottom side support low down and one 15-3/4" 1x2 top side support flush with the leg tops, which makes each finished set 18-3/4" across the outside faces of its legs. Use the plan's two-setting rule: 1-1/2" pocket holes with 2-1/2" screws where the 2x2 bottom support meets the 2x2 legs, and 3/4" holes with 1-1/4" screws for the 3/4"-thick 1x2 top support. Clamp both sets on the same flat surface and mark the bottom support's height across all four legs at once — the source does not name that height, so it is your call, but if the two sets disagree nothing you build on top of them will sit level. Glue every joint and check each set across the diagonals before the glue sets.

Stand the two leg sets 48" apart and join them with the two 48" 1x2 top front/back supports, one at the front and one at the back, flush with the leg tops. Those supports fit between the legs, so the finished frame is 51" long over the legs and 18-3/4" deep — the numbers the 51-1/2" x 19-1/4" tabletop is sized against, giving a 1/4" overhang all round. There is only one 48" 2x2 bottom support in the cut list, so it can only go on one face; put it at the back, level with the 2x2 bottom side supports, and join it with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws. Measure both diagonals across the top of the frame and rack it until they agree before the glue grabs, because every apron from here on references this rectangle.

Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of all five 1x4 aprons and along their top edges as well — the top-edge holes are what you will screw the tabletop down through later, and they are far easier to drill now than upside down under a finished desk. Fit the 48" back apron between the back legs, the two 15-3/4" side aprons between front and back legs, and the two 12" front aprons at the front, one at each end; 48" less two 12" aprons leaves a 24" clear opening for the drawer. Fasten everything with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue — 3/4" screws will not hold in 3/4" stock — insetting each apron 1/4" from the outside faces of the legs for the shadow line the design depends on.

Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends and along the top edges of the two 16-3/4" drawer supports, then fasten one inside each end of the drawer opening, running front to back behind the 12" front aprons, with 1-1/4" screws and glue. These are the vertical faces the 16" Euro slides screw to, so set them at exactly the same height on both sides — measure both down from the top of the aprons rather than up from the floor, and a slide that ends up 1/8" out of parallel with its mate will bind. Their inside faces need to finish 24" apart to match the opening, which is what gives the 23" drawer box its 1/2" of slide clearance per side.

Build the drawer box with the two 16" sides running the full front-to-back depth and the two 21-1/2" front and back pieces fitting between them; that makes the box 23" wide outside, which is exactly what the 16" x 23" plywood bottom is cut to. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" screws and glue, then glue and brad-nail the plywood bottom onto the underside of the box, using the sheet itself to square the box as you nail. Mount the 16" Euro slides bottom-corner style and hang the box — a 23" box in the 24" opening is exactly the 1/2" per side these slides want. Finally hold the 23-3/4" x 3" drawer face on with a 1/8" gap down each side of the 24" opening and along its bottom edge, leaving the larger gap at the top where the tabletop lip hides it; tack it with two brads, pull the drawer out, and fix it permanently with 1-1/4" screws driven from inside the box.

Remove the drawer, lay the 51-1/2" x 19-1/4" tabletop face down on a blanket and set the frame upside down on it. For a single desk, centre the frame so the top overhangs 1/4" on all four sides. If you are building the convertible pair, slide the frame instead so the top finishes flush with the back edge and with the side that will hinge, and put the whole 1/2" of overhang on the other two edges — a top that projects past the hinge line stops the two desks closing face to face, which is the one detail that makes the conversion work. Drive 1-1/4" screws up through the apron and 1x2 support pocket holes into the underside of the top, with a little glue, checking square as you go. Stand it up, refit the drawer, sand everything and ease the edges, then mount the two hinges along the back legs so the pair folds open into one table.

baseStepTitles: Cap the headboard and shape the header
Notch the leg bottoms for the rails
flags: REAL SHORTFALL, stated in prose: the 1x3 parts (two panel sides at 18-1/2", two at 9", two top caps at 30") total 115" and packBoards puts them on 2 boards. The materials list declares ONE 8 ft 1x3. The existing cut step already said 'two 8 ft 1x3' — it is the materials line that is wrong. All other stock verified sufficient: 1x2 10=10, 1x4 2=2, 1x6 2=2.

DEFECT FIXED IN PROSE, in EVERY affected step: two steps called for '2" nails'. The materials list holds only 'Brad nails, 1-1/4"' and 'Wood screws, 2"' — there is no 2" nail in this build. Both occurrences routed to the 2" wood screws.
DERIVED, not authored: the slat gap. Eighteen 1-1/2" slats use 27" of the 53-1/2" cleat, leaving 26-1/2" over seventeen spaces = about 1-9/16".

Verified sound and used as a build check: six 3-1/2" panel boards (21") plus two 2-1/2" 1x3 sides = 26", matching the 26" bottom trim, header, endplate and slats; 26" panel plus two 1-1/2" inside legs = the 29" top-of-panel piece; the 6" inside bottom leg plus the 18-1/2" panel plus the 5-1/2" header = exactly the 30" full-leg length.
Cleat HEIGHT on the rail is not specified in the source — the rewrite says to set both at the same height low enough that the mattress clears, without naming a figure.

ops.body: Cut six headboard center panel at 18-1/2" and six footboard center panel at 9" from the 1x4. Cut two headboard panel sides at 18-1/2", one headboard top cap at 30", two footboard panel sides at 9" and one footboard top cap at 30" from the 1x3. Cut one headboard bottom trim at 26", two headboard inside legs at 18-1/2", two headboard full legs at 30", one headboard top of panel/legs at 29", two headboard inside bottom legs at 6", two footboard trim at 26", four footboard legs at 15", one footboard top of panel/legs at 29", two cleats at 53-1/2" and 18 slats at 26" from the 1x2. Cut one headboard header at 26", two side rails at 54-1/2" and one endplate at 26" from the 1x6. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 1x4, two 8 ft 1x3, ten 8 ft 1x2 and two 8 ft 1x6. Note that the materials list calls for only one 8 ft 1x3 — the six 1x3 parts add up to 115", so buy two. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

Lay the six 18-1/2" 1x4 center panel boards edge to edge on a flat surface — six boards at 3-1/2" wide make a 21" field — then add a 2-1/2" 1x3 panel side against each outer edge to bring the panel to exactly 26" wide, which is the width of the 26" bottom trim, the 26" header and the 26" endplate further on. Run the 26" bottom trim across the face at the bottom of the field and glue and brad-nail through it into every one of the eight boards with 1-1/4" brads; that trim is the only thing tying the loose planks into one panel, so do not skip the glue. Hold the top ends of all eight boards against a straightedge as you nail — if they wander, the panel will not meet the header squarely and the error shows across the whole headboard.

Fasten an 18-1/2" inside leg to each side of the panel with 2" wood screws, driving into the 1x3 panel sides and into the ends of the bottom trim and header. Then glue and nail a 30" full leg over each inside leg with 1-1/4" brads, so each leg reads as one solid 1-1/2" post from the floor to the top of the headboard — building them up in two layers is what gives a 3/4" board the look of solid stock. Use the 2" wood screws wherever a longer fastener is wanted: the only nails in this build are 1-1/4" brads, and a 1-1/4" brad will not cross a 3/4" leg and still hold in the piece behind it. As a check on your work, the 6" inside bottom leg, the 18-1/2" panel and the 5-1/2" header stack to exactly the 30" full-leg length.

Fasten the 29" top of panel/legs piece across the top of the assembly with 2" wood screws — 26" of panel plus a 1-1/2" inside leg at each side is exactly 29", so if it does not drop in, something below is out. Add the 30" 1x3 top cap over it, overhanging the 29" width by 1/2" at each end. If you want the arched look, lay out and cut the curve on the 26" 1x6 header with a jigsaw before it goes in, sand the sawn edge smooth, and fit it between the inside legs above the panel; for a square top, substitute a 1x2 as the cut list notes and cut a second 26" bottom trim instead. Finish by gluing and nailing the two 6" inside bottom legs into the bottom of each leg stack, where they build the leg out to full thickness down to the floor.

The footboard is the same sequence at a shorter height: six 9" 1x4 center boards make a 21" field, a 2-1/2" 1x3 panel side on each edge brings it to 26", and the two 26" footboard trim pieces run across the face. Screw one 15" leg to each side of the panel with 2" wood screws, then glue and nail the second 15" leg of each pair over the first with 1-1/4" brads, so the footboard legs build up in two layers exactly like the headboard's — four 15" legs is two per side. Cap it with the 29" top of panel/legs piece and the 30" 1x3 top cap, the same 1/2" overhang at each end. Stand the headboard and footboard side by side before you go near the rails and confirm both measure 26" across the panel and 29" across the legs; the 26" endplate and the 26" slats depend on it.

Measure your actual 1x6 before marking anything — nominal 1x6 should be 5-1/2" wide, but if yours runs narrow the notch has to follow the board, not the drawing. Lay out a notch in the bottom of each leg the exact width of your rail stock and 3/4" deep, so the 54-1/2" side rails seat flush with the outside faces of the legs, and cut it with a jigsaw, staying just inside your line and paring back to it. Test-fit a rail offcut in every notch before you commit: a notch cut 1/16" oversize leaves the rail loose, and a loose rail is exactly how a bed develops a rack you can never screw out of it. Glue and 1-1/4" brads hold the rail in its notch while the screws in the next step do the structural work.

Stand the headboard and footboard upright and set the two 54-1/2" side rails into their notches. Fasten the 26" 1x6 endplate between the rails with 2" wood screws driven through the rails into its ends, then fasten the endplate to the footboard with 1-1/4" screws, and tie the rails to the headboard the same way. The 26" endplate is what sets the frame's clear inside width, and that clear width is the 26" your eighteen slats are cut to — so check the rail-to-rail measurement at both ends of the bed and in the middle before the last screw goes in. Measure the diagonals across the finished frame and adjust until they match; this is the last chance to square the bed before the cleats fix everything in place.

Glue and brad-nail the two 53-1/2" 1x2 cleats along the inside face of each side rail with 1-1/4" brads, both at the same height and low enough that the crib mattress sits down inside the rails rather than perched on top of them; the cleat is 1" shorter than the 54-1/2" rail, so centre it and leave about 1/2" clear at each end. Lay the eighteen 26" slats across the cleats and drive a 1-1/4" screw through each end into the cleat — screwing them down rather than resting them is what stops an active toddler kicking them loose, and it is the one instruction in this plan not to take as optional. Eighteen slats at 1-1/2" wide use 27" of the 53-1/2" cleat, leaving 26-1/2" spread over seventeen gaps, so about 1-9/16" between slats: set the two end slats tight to the ends and cut an offcut to that width as a gauge. Fill the nail holes with putty, sand every edge and corner round — there is no such thing as too much easing on a toddler's bed — and finish with a kid-safe finish.

baseStepTitles: Build the front and back double sections
flags: NO SHORTFALL — stated as sufficiency in the prose. packBoards puts the 149 1x4 pieces (4581-1/4" total) on 51 boards against the 52 declared, and the 18 2x3 pieces on 5 against 5 declared. One spare 1x4, no spare 2x3.
Verified: the 12-3/4"/25-1/2"/38-1/4"/51"/63-3/4" rail marks close on the 70-3/4" full-height leg — 70-3/4 less 63-3/4 is the stated 7" from the bottom.

The source does not distribute the 36 bolts across the individual verticals ('four bolts in each of the three full-height verticals, and so on per the plan'). The rewrite states the total (32 at 3" plus 4 at 2-1/2") and the placement rule, and does not invent a per-vertical count.
Rewrite kept deliberately light per the plan's existing quality: only the cut tally, the bolt-hole step and the final assembly step were touched.

ops.body: Cut twelve full-height legs at 70-3/4", four single-end legs at 45-1/4", four hanging end support at 43-1/2", four hanging mid-bunk step at 29", six feet at 3-1/2", two single-end feet at 29", two double-end filler at 47-1/2", two hanging support at 22", two hanging bottom end at 1-3/4", 28 filler between steps and rails at 9-1/4", two ladder top at 12-3/4", six side rails at 59-1/2", six foot-section rails at 21", two top safety rails at 61-1/4", four fourth step & mid-bunk side rail at 22-3/4", two second-mid step at 42", 18 end rails at 39", twelve inner-vertical supports at 2-1/2", 27 slats at 40-1/4" and four foot-section slats at 39" from the 1x4. Cut two lower slat supports at 54", two upper slat supports at 34-3/4" and 14 cross slat supports at 15-1/2" from the 2x3. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to 51 8 ft 1x4 and five 8 ft 2x3 — 51 of the 52 boards the materials list declares, so there is one spare 1x4 and no spare 2x3 at all. Set a stop block on the saw before you start: the 28 fillers at 9-1/4", the 27 slats at 40-1/4" and the 18 end rails at 39" are 73 of the 149 1x4 pieces on their own, and a stop block is the only realistic way to make them identical. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

Drill every connection hole about 1" below the bed rail it serves, plus one below each top safety rail, so the nut and washer end up tucked directly under a rail instead of sitting proud where a child can catch a knee on it. Clamp the two panels that will bolt together in their finished relationship and drill both at once — the hole has to line up through both faces, and a hole drilled from one side alone and then transferred by eye will not. That accounts for all 36 bolts: 32 of the 3" and four of the 2-1/2". Put a washer under the head and under the nut on every one, so the bolt does not crush the pine as it draws up and then go slack a month later. Before you drill anything, check each hole position against the 12-3/4" rail marks you laid out on the panels — a bolt hole is not something you get to move.

Lay the 2x3 slat supports in place first — the two 54" lower supports, the two 34-3/4" upper supports and the fourteen 15-1/2" cross supports, eight to the middle bunk, four to the upper and two to the lower as the cut list notes. Then build each bunk's slats into a mat: fasten the slats to one another rather than screwing them down to the supports, so the mat holds its spacing but still lifts straight out — screwing them to the supports would kill the knockdown feature that this whole bolted design exists for. The 27 40-1/4" slats serve the three main sleeping areas and the four 39" foot-section slats fill the shorter foot bays. Stand the end panels, the two inner cross sections and the front and back sections up and draw them together with the bolts, working from the bottom bunk upward and checking the frame stands plumb before you fully tighten anything. Fit both 61-1/4" top safety rails last, and do not leave them off — on a bunk this tall they are the difference between a bed and a fall.

flags: REAL SHORTFALL, stated in prose: the 2x2 parts (two 45" back legs, two 29-1/4" front legs, two 15" and two 16" stretchers) total 210-1/2". packBoards returns 3 boards with a provable lower bound of 3, against the TWO 8 ft 2x2 declared. Other stock verified sufficient: 1x2 1=1, 1x3 1=1, 1x4 2=2.

DEFECT FIXED IN PROSE: the materials list declares only EIGHT 'Pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"'. Those are routed to the four 2x2 stretchers ONLY (the sole 1-1/2"-into-1-1/2" joints); the aprons and backrest are 3/4" stock and take 1-1/4" screws, since a 2-1/2" screw exits the far face of a 1-1/2" leg. Even so, eight stretcher ends at two screws each needs sixteen 2-1/2" screws against the eight declared — stated in the prose.

DEFECT FIXED: the 'Build the seat frame' step tagged 'Pine, 1x3, 3 ft'. The front and side aprons are 3-1/2" wide, i.e. 1x4. The 1x3 in this build is the two 15" backrest apron pieces. Material tag corrected to 'Pine, 1x4, 8 ft'.

DATA INCONSISTENCY, stated in prose and not resolved: the side aprons are 16" and the front/back aprons 15", but the side stretchers are 15" and the front/back stretchers 16" — the lengths are transposed between the two tiers even though both span the same pairs of legs. The rewrite tells the builder to dry-fit rather than picking a side.

The source never attaches the backrest to the stool — it is built in step 2 and then never mentioned again. A 'Mount the backrest' step is INSERTED before the finish step. The 15-3/4" of back leg above the front legs is sound arithmetic (45" less 29-1/4").

UNPLACED PARTS, flagged not invented: the eight 3-1/2" seat slat end caps and the two 15" backrest apron pieces are cut but never installed by any step, and their positions are not determined by the data.
The box-geometry solver reports no resolvable box for this plan, so no claim is made about which member runs full length at the seat frame.

ops.body: The cut list names every part simply Pine, so sort it by cross-section before you touch the saw. From the 8 ft 1x2 cut four backrest slats at 8", two backrest sides at 7-1/4" and one backrest top rail at 15". From the 3 ft 1x3 cut two backrest apron pieces at 15". From the 1x4 cut two front aprons at 15", two side aprons at 16", five seat slats at 18" and eight seat slat end caps at 3-1/2". From the 2x2 cut two back legs at 45", two front legs at 29-1/4", two side stretchers at 15" and two front/back stretchers at 16". Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, the 1x2, 1x3 and 1x4 all come out of what the materials list declares — one 8 ft 1x2, one 3 ft 1x3 and two 8 ft 1x4 — but the 2x2 parts total 210-1/2", which cannot come out of two 8 ft boards however you arrange them, so buy three. Square every cut and label each piece; the 15" and 16" pieces appear in both the aprons and the stretchers and are easy to mix up.

The backrest is a small frame you build flat on the bench and add to the stool later: the 15" 1x2 top rail across the top, a 7-1/4" 1x2 side under each end, and the four 8" 1x2 slats spanning between them, spaced evenly across the 15" width. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and join it with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue — the 2-1/2" screws in the materials list would blow straight out through the face of 3/4" stock and are reserved for the 2x2 stretchers later. If you want a softer profile than a square top, shape the upper edge of the top rail with a jigsaw now, while the frame is still off the stool and easy to clamp. Check it square across the diagonals before the glue sets; this assembly ends up at eye level and any twist in it is the first thing anyone sees.

Stand the two 45" back legs and the two 29-1/4" front legs up and join them with the 1x4 aprons: the two 15" front aprons front and back, the two 16" side aprons side to side, all on edge and all set at the same height so the seat lands flat. Before you attach any of them, drill 3/4" pocket holes along the top inside edge of every apron facing UPWARD, as well as the usual holes in the ends for joining apron to leg — the upward holes are what let you screw the seat down from inside the frame later instead of lying under a finished stool trying to drive a screw overhead. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue; the aprons are 3/4" stock and a 2-1/2" screw would burst out through the outside face of a 1-1/2" leg. Check both diagonals across the top of the frame and confirm the two back legs stand parallel, since they carry the backrest. If the stool feels like it wants more, add corner braces now while you can still reach inside.

The four 2x2 stretchers tie the legs together lower down at footrest height — set them level all the way round and at the same height on both sides, measuring down from the underside of the aprons rather than up from the floor. Note before you drill that the stretcher lengths are the mirror of the aprons above: the aprons are 15" front and back and 16" side to side, while the cut list gives 15" side stretchers and 16" front/back stretchers. Dry-fit each stretcher in the gap it is supposed to span and use whichever one actually fits, rather than trusting the label. These are the only 1-1/2"-into-1-1/2" joints on the stool, so this is where the 2-1/2" pocket screws belong; drill 1-1/2" pocket holes for them, and drill into two different faces of each stretcher end rather than twice into the same face, which locks the joint against racking in both directions. That is eight stretcher ends, so at two screws each you need sixteen 2-1/2" screws against the eight the materials list declares.

Lay the five 18" 1x4 seat slats across the frame — five boards at 3-1/2" wide give 17-1/2" of solid seat — and shuffle them until the gaps between them are even and the outer two sit where you want them relative to the frame's outside faces. Screw each slat down from underneath through the upward-facing apron pocket holes you drilled earlier, using 1-1/4" pocket screws, and go easy with the glue so a cracked slat can be replaced later without destroying the frame. The cut list also gives eight 3-1/2" seat slat end caps, but no step in the source says where they go, so leave them aside and check the photo rather than fastening them somewhere they were never meant to be. Sit on the stool before you go any further: a slat that flexes is telling you about a screw you missed.

step.title: Mount the backrest
step.body: The two 45" back legs stand 15-3/4" above the 29-1/4" front legs, and that projecting length is what the backrest frame mounts to — the source builds the backrest but never tells you to attach it, so do it here. Its 15" top rail is the same span the front and back aprons set between the legs, so the frame should sit between the two back legs with its sides against their inside faces; clamp it in place, check it is level and parallel to the seat, then fasten through 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Set the height by sitting on the stool and finding where it lands comfortably against your back, since the source does not fix it. The two 15" 1x3 backrest apron pieces are the same 15" span again and clearly belong to this assembly, but no step places them — dry-fit them against the photo before committing.

baseStepTitles: Connect the frames and add slats
Build and attach the top frame
Add the lid's inner registration frame and hang it
flags: DATA CONTRADICTION, stated in prose and deliberately NOT resolved: the top frame is 28" x 17-1/2" outside in 2-1/2" stock, so its opening is 23" x 12-1/2". The lid inner frame's parts are 25-1/2" and 13", so its smallest possible outer footprint is 25-1/2" x 13" — too big to drop into the 23" x 12-1/2" opening and too small to register around the 28" x 17-1/2" frame. It fits neither way as drawn.

DEFECT FIXED: the 'Build and mount the shelf' step tagged 'Pine, 1x2, 8 ft'. The shelf (15" x 5-1/2") and both shelf supports (5-1/2" x 5-1/2") are 5-1/2" wide, i.e. 1x6. Corrected. The 1x2 in this build is the lid inner frame.

SECOND FIT PROBLEM, stated in prose: the lid top panel is four 5-1/2" boards plus one 2-1/2" edge piece = 24-1/2" of width against a 27" lid frame, so the panel cannot reach the frame ends. The source does not say where the 2-1/2" shortfall goes.
FASTENER GAP, stated in prose: the only pocket screw in the materials list is 1-1/4", which is short for the 2x2-into-2x2 frame joints (1-1/2" pocket holes normally take 2-1/2" screws).

Lumber verified SUFFICIENT, no shortfall: packBoards gives 2x2 4=4, 1x3 3=3, 1x6 2=2, 2x4 2=2, 1x2 1=1 — exactly the declared bill with no spare. The old cut step's tally ('four 8 ft Pine, three 8 ft Pine...') was correct in count but named no stock; corrected to real stock names.

Verified sound and used: 24" rails between two 1-1/2" legs = a 27" housing; 13-1/2" slats between the frames = 16-1/2" deep; against a 28" x 17-1/2" top frame that is a 1/2" overhang all round. The three side boards (5-1/2 + 5-1/2 + 2-1/2) sum to exactly the 13-1/2" side opening.
The plan has no curve-cutting tool in its list, so the shelf-bracket arch is written with a mitre-saw alternative rather than assuming a jigsaw.

ops.body: The cut list names every part simply Pine, so sort by stock first. From the 2x2 cut four legs at 35", six front/back frame rails at 24" and two front panel, lower at 10-3/4". From the 1x3 cut five back panel boards at 10-3/4", two side panel, lower at 8-3/4", two top frame ends at 28", two top frame sides at 17-1/2", two lid frame ends at 27", two lid frame sides at 16" and one lid top panel edge at 14-1/2". From the 1x6 cut four front panel boards at 10-3/4", four side panel boards at 8-3/4", four lid top panel boards at 14-1/2", two shelf supports at 5-1/2" and one shelf at 15". From the 2x4 cut the 14 side/shelf slats at 13-1/2". From the 1x2 cut two lid inner frame ends at 13" and two lid inner frame sides at 25-1/2". Cut the top-frame and lid-frame pieces last and at 45°, mitred for the top frame and bevelled for the lid, with every stated length taken long point to long point. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut that comes to four 8 ft 2x2, three 8 ft 1x3, two 8 ft 1x6, two 8 ft 2x4 and one 8 ft 1x2 — exactly what the materials list declares, with no spare on any of them. Measure your own cooler before you cut a single board and adjust these figures if it differs: the whole box is built around one specific footprint.

Each of the two main frames is two 35" 2x2 legs with three 24" frame rails between them — one at the top flush with the leg tops, one near the bottom, and one at the height you want the lower shelf. With the rails fitting between the legs, each frame comes out 27" wide overall and 35" tall, and both must match to the 1/16": mark all four legs together, clamped as a stack, so the rail positions cannot drift between the front and back frames. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the rail ends and glue every joint. Be aware that the only pocket screw in the materials list is 1-1/4", which is short for joining 1-1/2" stock — 2-1/2" pocket screws are what these joints really want, so add them or back the joints up with countersunk screws. Check both diagonals on each frame before the glue grabs.

Fasten the five 10-3/4" 1x3 back panel boards to the back frame, running them vertically with their faces on the outside of the frame. Five boards at 2-1/2" wide cover 12-1/2" of the frame's 27" width, so the back is meant to read as spaced slats rather than a solid panel — lay all five out across the frame and set the gaps even by eye before you fasten anything, and expect the gap to shift if your actual board widths run under nominal. Keep the top ends aligned against a straightedge and screw through each board into the rails behind it. This is the face that will end up against a wall, so it is also the forgiving one to practise your spacing on.

Fasten the four 10-3/4" 1x6 front panel boards to the front frame the same way, vertical and on the outside face. Four boards at 5-1/2" give 22" of coverage and the two 10-3/4" 2x2 lower front pieces add another 3", so the front and back are deliberately different — the front reads nearly solid against the back's open slats. Before you fasten the outermost boards, drill 3/4" pocket holes on their edges facing the 2x2 legs: the lower front pieces have to be attached in a corner you will not be able to reach once the panelling is on, and this is the moment that costs you nothing. Screw through into the rails, keeping the tops aligned.

Fit the two 10-3/4" 2x2 lower front pieces using the pocket holes you drilled in the previous step, or countersunk screws driven from inside while you can still get a driver in. Decide now how they sit relative to the 1x6 boards beside them: a 2x2 is 1-1/2" thick against the 1x6's 3/4", so they either stand proud as a deliberate detail or get set back flush, and whichever you choose both sides have to match or the front reads crooked. Glue as well as screw — a front panel that works loose will rattle every time the lid drops.

Stand the front and back frames parallel and join them with the 13-1/2" 2x4 side/shelf slats, which are what set the stand's depth: 13-1/2" between the frames plus a 1-1/2" leg at each side makes the housing 16-1/2" deep against 27" wide. All fourteen slats do double duty — some close in the sides, the rest form the lower shelf platform — and the source spaces them roughly 1-1/4" apart, so cut a 1-1/4" offcut and use it as a spacer instead of eyeballing each gap. Screw through the frame legs and rails into the ends of every slat, and check the assembly for square as you go; once more than a few slats are in, the box permanently holds whatever shape you gave it. Keep the run of slats consistent from side to side, because the side panel boards in the next step have to land on them.

Close in each side with two 8-3/4" 1x6 side panel boards and one 8-3/4" 1x3 side panel lower piece. Their widths are the check that the frame went together right: 5-1/2" plus 5-1/2" plus 2-1/2" is exactly 13-1/2", which is the clear span between the front and back frames, so on each side those three boards should fill the opening edge to edge with no gap at all. If they do not, your frames are not the 13-1/2" apart the slats set — stop and fix that before you fasten, because everything above depends on the housing being 27" x 16-1/2". Screw them to the legs and into the slats behind them.

Build the top frame flat on the bench from the two 28" ends and two 17-1/2" sides, all 1x3 mitred at 45° at both ends with those lengths taken long point to long point. Clamp the mitres tight, drill 3/4" pocket holes on the underside and pull each corner together with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue — putting the pocket holes underneath keeps the top face clean. The finished frame is 28" x 17-1/2" outside, which gives a 1/2" overhang all round over the 27" x 16-1/2" housing, and subtracting the 2-1/2" stock from each side leaves a 23" x 12-1/2" opening in the middle. Set it on, check the overhang is even on all four sides, and fasten it down with countersunk screws or nails. Then test-fit the cooler: it should drop through that opening and sit level before you spend any time on the lid.

Build the lid frame the same way from the two 27" ends and two 16" sides, 1x3 bevelled at 45° at both ends, long point to long point, so it closes as a 27" x 16" rectangle — smaller than the 28" x 17-1/2" top frame beneath it, sitting 1/2" in at each end and 3/4" in at each side. Then lay the four 14-1/2" 1x6 lid top panel boards and the single 14-1/2" 1x3 edge piece across the frame. Be warned that they do not close: four boards at 5-1/2" plus one at 2-1/2" is 24-1/2" of panel against the frame's 27" length, so the panel will fall 2-1/2" short and the source does not say where that shortfall goes. Decide before you glue anything — rip the edge piece wider from an offcut, or run an even reveal at both ends — rather than discovering it with the boards already down. Fasten the panel to the frame with glue and screws driven from underneath so the lid's top face shows no fasteners.

The inner frame glued to the underside of the lid is what registers the lid over the box opening and stops it sliding off-centre, so it has to be right before you touch a hinge. Be warned that the cut list does not close here: the top frame's opening is 23" x 12-1/2", but the lid inner frame's parts are 25-1/2" and 13", so its smallest possible outside size is 25-1/2" x 13" — too big to drop into that opening and too small to register around the outside of the 28" x 17-1/2" top frame. As drawn it fits neither way. Measure your own top frame opening instead, cut the inner frame to drop into it with about 1/8" of clearance all round, and treat the 25-1/2" and 13" figures as wrong rather than trying to force them. Once it drops in cleanly with the lid sitting centred, glue the inner frame to the lid's underside, let it cure fully, and only then hang the lid on its hinges along the back edge.

Shape the two 5-1/2" x 5-1/2" shelf supports into brackets: mark a curve on one, cut it, then use it as a template for the second so the pair matches exactly — or, if you would rather not saw a curve with the tools this plan lists, simply mitre a 45° corner off each block, which reads just as intentional. Sand the cut edge before it goes on, because you will not reach it afterwards. Fasten one bracket to each 2x2 leg at the shelf height with pocket screws or countersunk screws, both measured from the floor so the shelf cannot sit crooked, then set the 15" x 5-1/2" shelf on top of them and screw it down through into the brackets. Note that both the shelf and its brackets come from the 1x6, not the 1x2 — the 1x2 in this build is the lid's inner frame.

flags: NO numeric defect. packBoards confirms the 2x2 parts pack to 4 boards against 4 declared and the 1x3 parts to 4 against 4 declared. The existing cut step is correct and was left byte-identical.

ORPHANED PARTS — the real gap in this plan: the four 5-7/8" top gussets, the four 12" 2x2 cross braces and the four 23" 1x3 end caps are cut but installed by no step in the source. The rewrite gives each a home and says plainly, in the prose, where the placement is derived rather than authored.
The 23" end-cap placement (two per sawhorse along the top, over both 12-3/4" top pieces) is DERIVED from the 21-1/2" body length and the four-piece count, and is flagged as such in the step itself.
The 12" cross-brace position is NOT determined by the data; the rewrite says to dry-fit rather than asserting a location.
Verified sound and used: 18-1/2" horizontal supports between the leg assemblies plus a 1-1/2" leg at each end = 21-1/2", exactly the shelf-board length. Four 2-1/2" shelf boards = 10" of shelf.
Per the plan's own note, there are exactly four horizontal supports — two per sawhorse, a SINGLE tier. The rewrite says so explicitly so nobody reads a second tier into it.
FASTENER NOTE: the only pocket screw declared is 1-1/4", short for the 1-1/2" 2x2 leg joints; stated in the prose.

ops.body: Each sawhorse needs two leg pairs, so build four in all. A leg pair is two 25-7/8" leg pieces — both ends cut parallel at 15° off square — splayed under one 12-3/4" 2x2 top piece, whose ends are cut at 15° but not parallel and measured at the longest point. Orient each leg so the 15° cut at the bottom sits flat on the floor and the matching cut at the top meets the underside of the top piece cleanly; if a leg rocks, you have it end for end. Join with 1-1/2" pocket holes and screws, or with nails and glue as the plan allows — note that the only pocket screw in the materials list is 1-1/4", which is short for 1-1/2" stock, so glue every joint whichever way you fasten. Build all four pairs against the same layout marked on your bench and stand them side by side before the glue sets: a matched pair is the entire point of this plan, and a leg pair 1/4" taller than its mate will rock a desktop forever.

Glue and nail one 3" 1x3 inside block onto each leg pair, four in all. It sits on the inside of the assembly under the top piece and gives the angled leg joint a flat, square shoulder to bear against — without it you are asking the pocket screws alone to hold a splayed joint that wants to spread every time weight goes on the horse. Fit the four 5-7/8" 1x3 top gussets at the same time, one per leg pair: their ends are cut at 15° not parallel, longest point, which is exactly the angle that lets them lie flat across the leg-to-top joint and lock the splay. The source cuts these gussets but never says to install them, so this is where they belong. Use 1-1/4" brads and glue, and clamp each one until the glue grabs.

Stand two leg pairs facing each other and join them with two 18-1/2" 1x3 horizontal supports, one on each side, low down where the shelf will sit. There are exactly four supports in the cut list — two per sawhorse, a single tier, not two — so set both at the same height measured from the floor on all four legs, and clamp a spacer block under each while you fasten so it cannot creep. With 18-1/2" between the leg assemblies and a 1-1/2" leg at each end, each sawhorse finishes 21-1/2" long overall, which is exactly the length of the shelf boards; that number is your check that the supports went on square. Glue and nail with 1-1/4" brads. The four 12" 2x2 cross braces also belong somewhere in this assembly, two per sawhorse, but the source never places them — dry-fit them against the splayed legs, pick the position that best stiffens the horse against racking, and fasten only once you are satisfied. Then set both sawhorses side by side and compare heights and leg angles; this is the last easy moment to catch a mismatch.

Lay four 21-1/2" 1x3 shelf boards across the horizontal supports on each sawhorse, running the full 21-1/2" length of the assembly so their ends finish flush with the outside of the legs. Four boards at 2-1/2" wide give 10" of shelf across the span between the legs, so they will not fill it edge to edge — spread them evenly with an offcut as a spacer rather than pushing them all to one side and leaving a gap you will drop tools through. Glue and nail each board into both supports with 1-1/4" brads, two per end, keeping the outer boards parallel to the supports so the shelf does not read crooked from across the room.

step.title: Cap the tops
step.body: Finish each sawhorse with two 23" 1x3 end caps laid side by side along the top, spanning both 12-3/4" top pieces. At 23" they run 1-1/4" longer than the sawhorse's 21-1/2" body, so centre them and let the overhang fall evenly at each end; side by side the pair gives a 5"-wide bearing surface for a desktop, and a sacrificial 1x3 face is far better to saw into by accident than the 2x2 top piece underneath. The source cuts these four pieces but never places them, so this position is derived from their 23" length and the two-per-sawhorse count — check it against the photo before you nail. Glue and 1-1/4" brads are plenty. Sand the top faces flat and ease every edge so nothing catches a workpiece as you slide it across.

flags: CONFIRMED DEFECT: the description budgets 'three 35" door pieces' from the single 10 ft 2x4, but the cut list calls for FOUR door stiles at 35". Three 35" pieces plus kerfs use 105-1/4" of a 120" board; a fourth needs another 35" and does not fit. Stated in the prose — the fourth stile has to come out of the 8 ft 2x4 stock.

SHORTFALL CLAIMED, with the reason stated in prose: two 48" cross supports need 96-1/8" of a 96" board once you allow 1/8" for the kerf, so they do not pair. Four boards each take one 48" support plus one 47-1/2" rail, one takes a 48" plus the fourth 35" stile, and the remaining eight 48" supports need a board apiece — 13 8 ft 2x4 against the 9 declared. packBoards returns 13.

2x6 verified SUFFICIENT, no shortfall: four 144" runners are one whole 12 ft board each (4 = 4 declared) and seven 96" wall framing pieces are one whole 8 ft board each (7 = 7 declared). Neither leaves any margin for a mis-cut, which is said in the prose.

AMBIGUITY stated, not resolved: seven 96" wall framing pieces do not divide evenly between two walls, and the source does not say how they split. The rewrite tells the builder to lay them out on the ground against their own coop first.

AMBIGUITY stated, not resolved: four door stiles plus four door rails is enough for TWO door frames, and the materials list carries two gate hinge sets, yet every step speaks of one door. Flagged in the build-the-door step rather than picking a side.
The box-geometry solver reports no resolvable box for this plan, so no claim is made about which door member runs full length.

ops.body: From the 12 ft 2x6 cut the four 144" long wall runners — each uses a whole board end to end, so cut nothing else from these four and check your measurement twice, because there is no margin. From the 8 ft 2x6 cut the seven 96" wall framing pieces, again one per board with no waste. From the 8 ft 2x4 cut 13 cross supports at 48" and four door rails at 47-1/2". From the 10 ft 2x4 cut three door stiles at 35" — three is all a 120" board yields once you allow for the kerf, and the cut list calls for four, so the fourth 35" stile has to come out of the 8 ft 2x4 stock. Be warned about that stock too: two 48" cross supports need 96-1/8" of a 96" board once you allow 1/8" for the saw, so they do not pair up. Four boards will each take one 48" support plus one 47-1/2" rail, one will take a 48" plus the fourth stile, and the remaining eight 48" supports need a board apiece — 13 8 ft 2x4 in all, against the nine the materials list declares. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

Build each long wall as a flat frame on the ground before you stand anything up — a framed wall assembled flat and then raised is the whole reason this run holds its shape where a post-and-wire fence would not. The 96" 2x6 wall framing pieces are the wall's main members and the 48" 2x4 cross supports run between them, spaced evenly down the wall so the chicken wire has something solid to bite into every few feet rather than only at the corners. Note that seven 96" pieces will not divide evenly between two walls, and the source does not say how they split — lay them out on the ground against your own coop wall and decide before you fasten anything, since the odd board most likely becomes a centre post. Join with 2-1/2" pocket screws through the cross-support ends into the framing, and check the diagonals on each wall; every 48" cross support must be the same length, so measure a few against each other first.

Run the four 144" 2x6 long wall runners across the tops and ends of the two wall frames to tie the whole thing into one rigid structure — at 12 ft each they set the run's finished length, and they are the pieces that stop the two walls folding toward each other. Get help lifting them; a 12 ft 2x6 is heavy and unwieldy on your own, and dropping one onto a finished wall frame will undo an hour's work. Fasten with 2-1/2" pocket screws into the wall framing, and before you drive the last few, check that the walls stand parallel and exactly 48" apart at both ends and in the middle: the 48" cross supports and the 47-1/2" door rails both depend on that width holding. Set the structure on level ground or on blocks, because a run that racks will never keep a door square.

Choose the bay where you want the access door and frame the opening into the structure with 2x4, fastened with 2-1/2" pocket screws. Size the rough opening from the door you are about to build rather than from a number: the door frame is 47-1/2" across its rails and 35" on its stiles, and you need that plus clearance for the hinge knuckle and a gap all round so it swings without binding. If you would rather not guess, build the door frame first and hold it up to the opening before you fix the framing. Check the opening square in both diagonals — a door hung in a racked opening will catch at one corner no matter how well the door itself is made — and put the hinge side against a solid vertical, not a mid-span cross support.

Roll the 36" chicken wire over the top and down the sides of the structure, working from one end and keeping it under light tension so it does not sag between supports. The roll is only 36" wide and the run is both taller and wider than that, so plan your overlaps deliberately: lap each course a couple of inches over the last and staple through both layers, rather than butting the edges where a raccoon can push a nose through the seam. Staple with 1/2" staples every few inches into the 2x6 framing, the 144" runners and every 48" cross support — the cross supports exist precisely so the wire has an anchor mid-span instead of only at the perimeter. Leave the door opening clear, trim the wire back to the framing around it, and fold or staple down any cut ends so nothing sharp is left at chicken or hand height.

Build the door flat on a level surface as its own square unit — that is why it gets pocket holes rather than being a wire-covered hole in a wall panel. Take two 35" stiles and two 47-1/2" rails, drill 2-1/2" pocket holes in the rail ends, clamp the frame flat, and measure both diagonals before you drive a single screw; a door is the one part of this build where out-of-square shows the moment you hang it. Note before you start that the cut list gives four stiles and four rails, which is enough for two door frames, and the materials list carries two gate hinge sets — so decide whether you are building one door or two and check that against the opening you framed. Staple chicken wire across the front face of the finished frame with 1/2" staples and trim it flush. Hang the door on the gate hinges, then swing it through its full arc before you call it done and check it clears at every point, not just closed.

baseStepTitles: Attach the ends to the bottom
flags: GEOMETRY settled and stated: the ends butt to the ENDS of the 16" bottom, giving 16 + 3/4 + 3/4 = 17-1/2" overall — which is exactly what the 17-1/2" slats require — and a 4"-tall crate. The alternative reading (ends sitting on top of the bottom) gives a 16" crate with the slats overhanging 3/4" at each end and a 4-3/4" height; the rewrite names it and rules it out.

ZERO-KERF SHORTFALL, real and stated: the three 1x6 parts (4" + 4" + 16") total exactly 24", which is the whole declared 2 ft board with nothing left for the saw. Flagged as buy-a-little-extra rather than as a wrong dimension.
Lath verified sufficient: four 17-1/2" slats use 70" of the declared 6 ft, packBoards 1 board.

DERIVED, not authored: with a 4" crate and a 3/4" bottom there is 3-1/4" of side above the bottom, and two 1-1/2" slats cover 3" of it — so the slat gap is small and the rewrite says to place the lower slat against the bottom and the upper flush with the top.
Kept short deliberately: this is a difficulty-1 project and the depth target is sufficiency, not padding. The final finishing step was left byte-identical.

ops.body: Cut two ends at 4" and one bottom at 16" from the 1x6, and four side slats at 17-1/2" from the lath. Those three 1x6 pieces total exactly 24", which is the whole 2 ft board with nothing left over for the saw kerf — start from a longer offcut or buy a little extra, or the second end will finish a shade under 4". The four 17-1/2" slats use 70" of the 6 ft of lath, which leaves plenty of margin. Square every cut: on a crate this small an out-of-square end shows immediately, and there is no joinery here to hide it.

Stand the two 4" end pieces on edge at each end of the 16" bottom and butt them against the bottom's end grain, so each end runs the full height and the bottom fits between them. That makes the crate 17-1/2" long overall — 16" plus 3/4" of end at each end — which is exactly what the 17-1/2" slats are cut to, and it makes the finished crate 4" tall with 3-1/4" of usable depth above the 3/4" bottom. Do not sit the ends on top of the bottom instead: that reading gives a 16" crate and every slat will overhang by 3/4" at each end. Glue both joints and drive three or four 1-1/4" brads through each end into the bottom, holding the end square to the bottom as you nail since end grain gives a brad very little to grip.

Run two 17-1/2" lath strips along each side, glued and nailed to the outside faces of both end pieces so their ends finish flush with the ends of the crate. Two 1-1/2" strips cover 3" of the 3-1/4" of side available above the bottom, so set the lower strip down against the bottom board and the upper flush with the top of the ends, and let the small gap fall in the middle — that gap is what gives the crate its slatted look. The lath is only 1/4" thick and splits at the drop of a hat, so keep the brads back from the ends, dial the nailer pressure down, and use staples if you have them. Check the crate sits flat on the bench before the glue sets.

flags: REAL SHORTFALL, hand-proved and stated in prose: the 2x2 parts (six 45", four 32", six 11-1/4") total 465-1/2". The best packable combination on a 96" board is 45 + 32 + 11-1/4 = 88-1/2" with kerfs, and once the six 45" pieces are placed the remainder does not redistribute — five boards cannot hold it. packBoards returns 6 (lower bound 5, not provable). Six 8 ft 2x2 against the five declared. The existing cut step already said 'six' while the materials line says five; the materials line is the error.

DEFECT FIXED IN PROSE: the source used the single 'Pocket hole screws, 1-1/4" and 2-1/2"' line indiscriminately. Shelf stock is 3/4", so those joints take the 1-1/4" screws; a 2-1/2" screw driven into a 1-1/2" 2x2 leg exits the far face — the visible outside of the finished piece. Only the 2x2-into-2x2 end-panel joints take 2-1/2".

REALLOCATION, stated: the source's 'attach the shelf trim to the shelf fronts and ends' cannot be right — all six shelf trim pieces are 45", and the six 11-1/4" shelf end trim pieces are exactly consumed by the two end panels at three rails each. So the shelves get 45" trim front and back only.
Shelf HEIGHTS are not specified anywhere in the source (legs are 32", three shelves, no spacing given). The rewrite says so and tells the builder to transfer identical marks between the two panels.
1x12 verified sufficient: three 45" shelves use 135" of the declared 12 ft board, packBoards 1 board.
The box-geometry solver reports no resolvable box for this plan, so no claim is made about which member runs full length at the shelf-to-panel joint.
The 'Finish' step was left byte-identical — it is short but adequate, and padding it would be manufacturing work.

ops.body: Cut three shelves at 45" from the 12 ft 1x12; at 11-1/4" the board needs no ripping, and the three shelves use 135" of the 144" board. From the 2x2 cut six shelf trim at 45", four legs at 32" and six shelf end trim at 11-1/4". Those 2x2 parts total 465-1/2", and they will not come out of five 8 ft boards: the best you can pack onto one 96" board is a 45" plus a 32" plus an 11-1/4", which is 88-1/2" with the kerfs, and once the six 45" pieces are placed the leftovers do not redistribute far enough to close the gap. Buy six 8 ft 2x2 rather than the five the materials list declares. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — the 45" trim and the 45" shelves are easy to confuse in a leaning stack, and one is 11-1/4" wide while the other is 1-1/2" square.

Trim each of the three shelves front and back with a 45" 2x2, one on each long edge, keeping the top of the trim dead flush with the top face of the shelf. Because the 2x2 is 1-1/2" deep and the shelf only 3/4" thick, the trim hangs 3/4" below the shelf — that projection is the stiffener that keeps a 45" span of 1x12 from sagging under a row of books, and it is also what gives the Parsons look its thick edge. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the underside of each shelf facing outward into the trim, and fasten with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue; the shelf is 3/4" stock, so the 2-1/2" screws in the same box would drive straight out through the face of the trim. Clamp each shelf and its trim down to a flat surface while you drive the screws, or the assembly will pull into a bow you cannot straighten later. Note that the six 11-1/4" shelf end trim pieces are NOT used here — they are the rails of the two end panels in the next step, which is exactly what their count of six allows.

Each end panel is two 32" 2x2 legs with three 11-1/4" shelf end trim pieces between them, one rail at the height of each shelf — the two panels together use all four legs and all six end trim pieces. The source does not fix the shelf spacing anywhere, so that is your call: mark both legs of a panel at once with the panel clamped as a pair, then transfer those same marks to the second panel rather than measuring it fresh, because the two ends only have to agree with each other. These are the only 2x2-into-2x2 joints in the whole build, so this is where the 2-1/2" pocket screws belong; drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the rail ends and glue every joint. Check each panel across the diagonals and then lay one on top of the other face to face to confirm they match before you go any further — a 1/8" disagreement here becomes a shelf that will not sit level.

Lay one end panel flat with its inside face up, stand the three trimmed shelves on their rails, and screw through into the shelf ends; then lower the second panel on and repeat. The Parsons detail is that the end panels finish flush with the front edge and the top of the shelves — no setback, no reveal, no leg standing proud — so pull each shelf tight to the front of its rail before you fasten it, and sight down the front face to confirm it reads as one unbroken plane. Use the 2-1/2" screws only where 2x2 meets 2x2, and 1-1/4" screws anywhere a screw is going into 3/4" shelf stock: a 2-1/2" screw driven into a 1-1/2" leg comes out the far face, which on this piece is the visible outside. Stand it up, check it for level and for rack, and adjust or shim before the glue sets — with three fixed shelf levels there is no adjustment left afterwards.
